elements.sent-2411-001: We consider hypergraph visualizations that represent vertices as points in the plane and hyperedges as curves passing through the points of their incident vertices.
elements.sent-2411-002: Specifically, we consider several different variants of this problem by (a) restricting the curves to be lines or line segments, (b) allowing two curves to cross if they do not share an element, or not; and (c) allowing two curves to overlap or not.
elements.sent-2411-003: We show $\exists\RR$-hardness for six of the eight resulting decision problem variants and describe polynomial-time algorithms in some restricted settings.
elements.sent-2411-004: Lastly, we briefly touch on what happens if we allow the lines of the represented hyperedges to have bends---to this we generalize a counterexample to a long-standing result that was sometimes assumed to be correct.
elements.sent-2411-005: Hypergraphs, or equivalently set systems, arise in many domains and visualizing them is a non-trivial challenge.
elements.sent-2411-006: Classical approaches, such as Venn and Euler diagrams~\cite{jp-hpcdvd-87,amahmr-sv-16,m-dh-90 do not scale to large instances.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Recent experimental work~\cite{wallinger2021readability has shown that representing hypergraphs as collections of polylines (for the hyperedges) and common intersection points (for the vertices) allows for faster and more accurate performance of hypergraph-related tasks.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{./graphics/The_Simpsons_metro_small.pdf} \caption{A visualization of a Simpsons hypergraph dataset using the MetroSets metaphor \cite{jacobsen2020metrosets}. The visualization is taken from \url{https://metrosets.ac.tuwien.ac.at/}. Hyperedges are represented by metro lines and elements are represented by stations.} \label{fig:metrosets}
elements.sent-2411-008: In particular, the LineSets~\cite{ahrc-dslnvt-11 and the MetroSets approach~\cite{jacobsen2020metrosets use the metro map metaphor, where each hyperedge is a metro line and each vertex an interchange station.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: While LineSets connects pre-embedded vertices with arbitary curves, MetroSets optimizes vertex positions and aims to visualize the result in an octolinear style; see \cref{fig:metrosets .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Minimizing the visual complexity (which depends on the total number of bends along the metro lines), makes the representations simpler to understand and work with.
elements.sent-2411-011: A natural question is: which hypergraphs can be represented with just one bendless line segment per hyperedge? With this in mind, we study the problem of representing a hypergraph with vertices as points in the plane and hyperedges as straight lines through incident vertices.
elements.sent-2411-012: Specifically, we consider several problems by varying one of the following requirements (see also \cref{fig:representations and formal definitions in \cref{section:preliminaries ):.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The curves must be line segments or (infinite) lines.
elements.sent-2411-014: Two curves are allowed to cross if they do not share an element, or not.
elements.sent-2411-015: Two curves are allowed to overlap (i.e., to share a line segment), or not (called strict).

elements.sent-2411-016: In an extensive complexity study, we investigate for which hyperedge cardinalities and vertex degrees each of the eight problems are $\exists\RR$-hard or solvable in polynomial time.
elements.sent-2411-017: We also study special graph classes that always admit such representations.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our results are structured by distinguishing representations, where crossings are permitted (\cref{section:representations , \cref{table:resultswcrossings ) or not permitted (\cref{section:crossfreerepresentations , \cref{table:resultswocrossings ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: We start with preliminaries (\cref{section:preliminaries ) and a description of the Pappus configuration (\cref{section:pappus ) as an essential tool for constructing gadgets in our reductions.}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{./graphics/intro-big.pdf} \caption{(a) A linear hypergraph $H$. (b) A strict line representation of $H$. (c) A strict segment representation of $H$. (d) A crossing-free strict segment representation of $H$. (e)--(f) A hypergraph and its non-strict segment representation.} \label{fig:representations}
elements.sent-2411-021: Representing hypergraphs with one line per hyperedge relates to classical geometric problems dating back to the 19th century, particularly in the study of configurations~\cite{DBLP:books/ph/Gruenbaum09 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: A {\em combinatorial (or {\em geometric ) {\em configuration is an abstract (or Euclidean) incidence structure with points and lines where each point is incident to the same number of lines, and each line to the same number of points.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: A {\em realization of a combinatorial configuration is a geometric configuration, i.e., an embedding of the points and lines in the Euclidean plane that contains exactly the incidences that appear in the configuration.}
elements.sent-2411-024: This was central to Steinitz's PhD thesis~\cite{steinitz1894konstruction and studied in notable configurations like those of Desargues, Pappus, and Möbius–Kantor~\cite{DBLP:books/ph/Gruenbaum09 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Steinitz~\cite{steinitz1894konstruction claimed that in a 3-uniform 3-regular linear hypergraph (where each point lies on three lines, each line passes through three points, and no two lines share more than one point), removing one point allows for a realization.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Gropp had already noted the connection between configurations and hypergraph drawings in his 1995 paper~\cite{g-dc-95 , arguing that using straight lines for hyperedges improves readability.}
elements.sent-2411-028: He later collected classical results and posed hypergraph realizability as an open question~\cite{DBLP:journals/dm/Gropp97 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Deciding whether the representation of a hypergraph can be a non-crossing straight-line drawing of a tree can be done in polynomial time\cite{DBLP:journals/siamcomp/SwaminathanW94 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: A support graph $G$ of a hypergraph $H$ is a graph with the same vertex set where each hyperedge of $H$ is connected in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Techniques for drawing hypergraphs via support graphs~\cite{bkmsv-psh-11a,bcps-psh-12,cgmny-spssh-19 have a different focus and do not take into account whether vertices along hyperedges are collinear.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Minimizing the number of crossings in a path-based support graph (hyperedges form a path in $G$) is \NP-hard~\cite{DBLP:conf/sofsem/FrankKKMPUW21 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The intersection graph of line segments~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/Matousek14 can be seen as a strict segment representations of a linear hypergraph.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Gonçalves \cite{DBLP:journals/ejc/Goncalves09 showed that some planar linear hypergraphs (see \cite{DBLP:journals/ejc/Goncalves09 for a definition) cannot be represented with straight line segments (in contrast to planar graphs).}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Segment contact representations of planar graphs can sometimes help in finding crossing-free strict line representations for linear hypergraphs.
elements.sent-2411-036: A necessary and sufficient condition for representing a graph as a contact system of segments is known~\cite{de2007representation,DBLP:journals/algorithmica/FraysseixM07 , but no polynomial-time algorithm exists to test it.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The hypergraph visualization problem is also similar to the stretchability problem~\cite{shor1991stretchability,s-csgtp-09 : given an arrangement of pseudolines, is there a combinatorially equivalent arrangement of lines? Unlike hypergraph representations, the order of vertices along each pseudoline is fixed in the stretchability problem.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Matroid representability~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2301-03221 is another related problem, asking whether elements of a matroid can be represented as vectors in~$\RR^3$ such that independent sets are retained, becoming similar to hypergraph representations through projective transformation onto the plane.}
elements.sent-2411-039: A hypergraph $H = (V,E)$ is defined by a vertex set $V$ and a hyperedge set $E$, \revision{where each $e\in E$ is a distinct non-empty subset of $V$.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The degree of a vertex $v$ in $H$ is the number of hyperedges containing~$v$.
elements.sent-2411-041: The rank of $H$ is the maximum cardinality of a hyperedge $|e|$ over all $e$ in $E$.
elements.sent-2411-042: A hypergraph $H$ is $k$-uniform if every hyperedge has cardinality $k$ and it is $k$-regular if every vertex has degree~$k$.
elements.sent-2411-043: It is linear if $|e\cap e'|\le 1$ for every pair of distinct hyperedges $e,e'\in E$.
elements.sent-2411-044: The dual hypergraph of $H$ is obtained by interchanging the role of vertices and hyperedges, i.e., the dual of $H=(V,E)$ is $H_d=(E,E')$ where $E'$ consists of hyperedges $\{e\mid v\in e\}$ for each $v\in V$.
elements.sent-2411-045: The hyperedge intersection graph $G$ of $H$ is a graph with $E$ as its vertex set, where two vertices are adjacent if and only if the corresponding hyperedges share a common element.
elements.sent-2411-046: A point $x$ is incident to a line segment/linesegment $\ell$ if and only if $\ell$ contains $x$.
elements.sent-2411-047: A line representation of a hypergraph consists of an injective mapping $\alpha$ of vertices to points in $\RR^2$ and an injective mapping $\beta$ of hyperedges to lines in $\RR^2$ such that $v\in e$ if and only if $\alpha(v)\in \beta(e)$ for $v\in V,e\in E$.
elements.sent-2411-048: A segment representation of a hypergraph is defined as a line representation with line segments instead of lines.
elements.sent-2411-049: A line representation/segment representation is strict if every pair of lines/line segments share at most one point.
elements.sent-2411-050: It is crossing-free if a pair of lines/line segments $\beta(e)$ and $\beta(e')$ in the representation share a point if and only if $e\cap e'\ne \emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-051: We are concerned with the problem of deciding whether a hypergraph has a representation.
elements.sent-2411-053: We often omit non-crossing-free and non-strict, i.e., by a segment representation we mean a non-strict non-crossing-free segment representation.
elements.sent-2411-054: For simplicity of our presentation, we disallow two hyperedges to contain the same set of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-055: With this in mind, let us point out the following observation, which helps combine some of our complexity results.
elements.sent-2411-056: If $H$ contains a pair of hyperedges $e,e'$ with $|e\cap e'|\ge 2$ and $e\ne e'$, then $H$ \revision{has no line representation, no strict line representation, no crossing-free line representation, and no strict crossing-free line representation.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Here the class $\exists \RR$ is a complexity class between \NP\ and \PSPACE\ that contains all problems that can be reduced to solving an existentially quantified formula of polynomial equations and inequalities \revision{with integer coefficients ; this means that $\exists\RR$-hardness implies \NP-hardness.}
elements.section-2411-003: Pseudoline Stretchability and the Pappus Configuration
elements.sent-2411-059: We often reduce from the $\exists\RR$-hard problem pseudoline stretchability~\cite{s-csgtp-09 to prove $\exists\RR$-hardness of our problems.}
elements.sent-2411-060: A {\em pseudoline is an $x$-monotone curve in $\mathbb{R ^2$ and a {\em simple pseudoline arrangement is a set of pseudolines where every pair of pseudolines intersects exactly once and no three pseudolines meet at a common point.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The pseudoline stretchability problem takes a simple pseudoline arrangement as input and seeks a combinatorially equivalent drawing where each pseudoline is drawn as a straight line segment, i.e., a homeomorphic line segment arrangement (see \cref{fig:pappusgadget (a)--(b)).}
elements.sent-2411-062: Many of our reductions make use of the Pappus hypergraph or Pappus configuration, and its ability to force three points to be collinear, as stated below.
elements.sent-2411-063: The Pappus hypergraph or Pappus gadget $H_P=(V_P,E_P)$ is given as $V_P=\{p_1,\dots,p_9\}$ and $E_P$ consists of the following hyperedges. \begin{equation}{l} \{p_1, p_2, p_3\}, \{p_4, p_5, p_6\}, \{p_1, p_4, p_8\}, \{p_1, p_5, p_9\}, \{p_2, p_4, p_7\}, \{p_2, p_6, p_9\}, \{p_3, p_5, p_7\}, \{p_3, p_6, p_8\} \end{equation} We call $\{p_1,\dots,p_6\}$ the fixers and $\{p_7,p_8,p_9\}$ the anchors.
elements.sent-2411-064: For a line representation of the Pappus gadget see \cref{fig:pappusgadget (c).}
elements.sent-2411-065: Notice that all hyperedges have size three, all fixers have degree three, and all anchors have degree two.
elements.sent-2411-066: The most important property is given next, and follows directly from the well-known Pappus theorem.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{./graphics/pappusgadget-new_small.pdf} \caption{(a) A simple pseudoline arrangement and (b) its stretched line segment representation. (c) A line representation of the Pappus gadget. The three anchors $p_7,p_8,p_9$ are always collinear. (d) ``Pushing'' the points $p_1$ and $p_3$ very close to $p_2$ forces all fixers $p_1$--$p_6$ to be inside a disk of arbitrarily small radius $\epsilon$.} \label{fig:pappusgadget}
elements.sent-2411-067: Next, we show that given a fixed embedding of the anchors, the remaining part of the Pappus gadget can be represented somewhat flexibly such that it can avoid a finite set of predefined points and lines.
elements.sent-2411-068: There exists a line representation $(\alpha,\beta)$ of $H_P$ with $\alpha(p_7)=\gamma_1,\alpha(p_8)=\gamma_2,\alpha(p_9)=\gamma_3$, where none of the lines in $\beta(E_P)$ passes through any of the points in $A$, and none of the fixers lie on any line in $B$.. \end{restatable.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Due to symmetries in the Pappus gadget we can assume that $\gamma_2$ lies on the line segment from $\gamma_1$ to $\gamma_3$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Now let $p$ be a point in the plane such that.
elements.sent-2411-071: We now argue that for any $\epsilon>0$, $H_P$ can be represented such that $\alpha(p_i)=\gamma(p_i)$ for $i=7,8,9$, and the fixers of $H_P$ are represented inside the disk of radius $\epsilon$ around $p$ (see \cref{fig:pappusgadget (c)).}
elements.sent-2411-072: Since $\gamma_2$ lies on the line segment from $\gamma_1$ to $\gamma_3$, we can represent $H_P$ such that the representation is homeomorphic to the one in \cref{fig:pappusgadget (c) and $p_2$ lies exactly on $p$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: This is because we can freely choose three collinear points defining the representation of $p_1,p_2$ and $p_3$ on a line different from the one through $p_7,p_8,p_9$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Together with the representation of $p_7,p_8,p_9$ this in turn defines the remaining representation of the Pappus gadget.
elements.sent-2411-075: By decreasing both the distance of $p_1$ to $p_2$ and $p_3$ to $p_2$ (i.e.\ pushing them towards $p_2$), all fixers of the Pappus gadget get closer to $\alpha(p_2)=x$ (see \cref{fig:pappusgadget (d)).}
elements.sent-2411-076: Thus, by making $\epsilon$ small enough, none of the following hyperedge representations contains any point of $A$, as they get ``closer and closer'' to the lines defined by $p$ and $\gamma_1,\gamma_2,\gamma_3$, respectively. \begin{equation}\{p_1,p_4,p_8\},\{p_1,p_5,p_9\},\{p_2,p_4,p_7\},\{p_2,p_6,p_9\},\{p_3,p_5,p_7\},\{p_3,p_6,p_8\}\end{equation} We choose a small $\epsilon$ such that the disk around $p$ with radius $\epsilon$ neither contains any point from $A$ nor intersects with any line from $B$.
elements.sent-2411-077: Lastly, we show that the two remaining hyperedges $\{p_1,p_2,p_3\}$ and $\{p_4,p_5,p_6\}$ can be represented such they do not contain any point of $A$.

elements.sent-2411-081: We show that it is $\exists\RR$-hard to decide whether there exists a line representation for a given rank-$3$ hypergraph $H$.
elements.sent-2411-083: For the purposes of a simple description, we give here a simplified description of a variant of that problem that is still $\exists\RR$-hard \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2301-03221 .}
elements.sent-2411-085: A matroid $M$ is given as $M=(X,\mathcal{I )$ where $X$ is the finite ground set and $\mathcal{I}\subseteq 2^X$ is the set of independent sets with.}
elements.sent-2411-086: A representation of $M$ is an injective mapping $f(X):X\to \RR^3$ such that for any $Y\subseteq X$ we have $Y\in \mathcal{I}$ if and only if $f(Y)$ forms a set of linearly independent vectors in $\RR^3$.
elements.sent-2411-087: The $\exists\RR$-hard problem Matroid Representability is given as input a matroid and the question is whether there is a representation $f$ of $M$.
elements.sent-2411-088: For the vectors $v\in\RR^3$ we call the first, second, and third coordinate the $x,y,$ and $z$-coordinates, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-089: We start by making some normalizations to $M$.
elements.sent-2411-090: First, we can assume that every independent set $I\in\mathcal{I}$ has cardinality of at most $3$, as there is otherwise no representation.
elements.sent-2411-091: Second, we can assume that each pair $\{x,x'\}\in {X\choose 2}$ forms an independent set, i.e.\ $\{x,x'\}\in\mathcal{I}$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Otherwise, $f(x)=cf(x')$ for some $c\in \RR$ must hold for any representation.
elements.sent-2411-093: We can remove $x'$ from $X$ and replace any occurrence of $x'$ in $\mathcal{I}$ by $x$, and obtain an equivalent instance w.r.t.\ representability.
elements.sent-2411-094: We are ready to prove the theorem.
elements.sent-2411-096: We are given a matroid $M=(X,\mathcal{I )$ (we assume both discussed normalizations were applied already) and transform it to a hypergraph $H=(V,E)$ as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-097: To construct $H$, we first add to $H$ the set $X$ as vertices.

elements.sent-2411-100: Otherwise, we add a new point $d$, force $d$ to be collinear with $x$ and $x'$ using a new Pappus gadget with anchors $d,x$ and $x'$, and lastly add the hyperedge $\{d,x''\}$ to $E$ (see \cref{fig:erstrictlinegadets (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-101: We argue that $H$ has a line representation if and only if $M$ has a representation: ``$\Rightarrow$'': let $(\alpha,\beta)$ be a line representation of $H$.

elements.sent-2411-133: Given an instance $I$ of pseudoline stretchability, we construct a hypergraph~$H$ by taking intersection points as vertices.

elements.sent-2411-169: Assume now that we are given a hypergraph $H=(V,E)$ without any of the two forbidden substructures.
elements.sent-2411-170: We show how to construct a segment representation.
elements.sent-2411-171: Let $E_a\subseteq E$ be defined as \begin{equation}E_a=\{e\in E\mid \forall e'\in E:e'\ne e\Rightarrow |e\cap e'|\le 1\},\end{equation} and let $E_b=E\setminus E_a$.
elements.sent-2411-172: In other words, a hyperedge belongs to $E_a$ if it has at most one vertex in common with any other edge in the hypergraph, and $E_b$ consists of the remaining set of hyperedges.
elements.sent-2411-173: Since $H$ is a max-degree-2 and rank-3 hypergraph, we can observe some structural properties of $E_b$.
elements.sent-2411-174: For each $e\in E_b$ there exists exactly one other $e'\in E_b$ with $|e\cap e'|=2$.
elements.sent-2411-175: Hence, we define $\text{sib (e)=e'$ and partition $E_b$ into pairs of siblings.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Now define the edge set $E_c$ which consists of $E_a$ plus for each sibling pair $\{x,x'\}$ it contains the hyperedge $p_{x,x' =(x\cup x')\setminus (x\cap x')$ (\cref{fig:fp (d)).}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Next, consider a line arrangement of $|E_c|$ line segments in general position such that each pair of line segments crosses exactly once.
elements.sent-2411-178: Assign to each hyperedge $e\in E_c$ a distinct line segment $s(e)$ from this arrangement.
elements.sent-2411-179: If $e=p_{x,x'}$ for a sibling pair $x,x'$, we set $s(x)=s(x')=s(e)$.
elements.sent-2411-181: Next, for each vertex $v\in V$ that is not incident to two hyperedges of a sibling pair, we let $\alpha(v)$ be the intersection point of the line segments $s(e)$ and $s(e')$ of its two incident hyperedges $e$ and $e'$ (\cref{fig:fp (f)).}
elements.sent-2411-182: We now find the representations for the sibling pairs.
elements.sent-2411-183: It suffices to consider a sibling pair $p_{x,x'}$ such that $|x\cup x'|=4$, otherwise the construction is trivial.
elements.sent-2411-184: For each sibling pair $x,x'$ we cover $s(p_{x,x' )$ by $\beta(x)$ and $\beta(x')$ as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Firstly, $\beta(x)$ and $\beta(x')$ need to share a non-empty segment that will contain the two points $\alpha(y)$ and $\alpha(y')$ for $y,y'\in x\cap x'$ (\cref{fig:fp (g)).}
elements.sent-2411-186: Since the line segments are in general position, $\alpha(y)$ and $\alpha(y')$ can be chosen such that no other line segment besides $\beta(p_{x,x' )$ contains them.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Next, if for example $x\setminus x'=\{v\}$, then we define $\beta(x)$ such that it contains $\alpha(v)$ and $\beta(x')$ such that it does not contain $\alpha(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-188: If $x'\setminus x=\{v'\}$ we define $\beta(x')$ such that it contains $\alpha(v')$ and $\beta(x)$ such that it does not contain $\alpha(v')$.

elements.section-2411-005: Representations without bends and without crossings
elements.sent-2411-198: In this section, we give some results on deciding the existence of crossing-free representations, which are summarized in \cref{table:resultswocrossings .}
elements.sent-2411-199: Again, the equivalence between strict and non-strict is because of \cref{observation:strictvnonstrict .}
elements.sent-2411-200: We have two positive results for rank-2 and max-degree-2 hypergraphs, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-201: The first follows due to an easy case distinction, the second by an equivalence of crossing-free line representability of a linear hypergraph $H$ (non-linear hypergraphs are not line representable) and its hyperedge intersection graph being a complete $k$-partite graph.
elements.sent-2411-203: We assume $H$ is linear, otherwise there is no such representation.
elements.sent-2411-205: If $H$ only contains vertices of degree one then $H$ clearly has a crossing-free line representation with parallel lines as it consists of pairwise independent hyperedges.
elements.sent-2411-206: Otherwise, let $v$ be a vertex of degree $d>1$ and let $e_1,\dots,e_d$ be its incident hyperedges.
elements.sent-2411-208: If $d=2$ then $H$ has a crossing-free line representation if and only if the set of hyperedges $E' = E\setminus\{e_1,e_2\}$ can be represented as a set of parallel lines without creating unnecessary adjacencies with the representations of $e_1$ and $e_2$.
elements.sent-2411-209: This implies that no two hyperedges in $E'$ share a common vertex, and exactly one of the following conditions hold: (a) Every hyperedge of $E'$ has a vertex in common with $e_1$.
elements.sent-2411-210: For (a) and (b), the hyperedges of $E'$ can be drawn parallel to $e_1$ and $e_2$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-211: For (c), the hyperedges of $E'$ can be drawn with parallel lines where all of them intersect the lines for $e_1$ and $e_2$.
elements.sent-2411-212: If $d\ge 3$ then $H$ cannot contain any hyperedge $e\not\in\{e_1,\dots,e_d\}$, because the representation of $e$ would intersect all the other lines implying a rank of at least 3.
elements.sent-2411-213: Then, in polynomial time, one can decide whether $H$ admits a crossing-free line representation.. \end{restatable.}
elements.sent-2411-214: If $H$ is not linear, then it does not have a crossing-free line representation.
elements.sent-2411-216: Any two hyperedges that share a vertex must intersect.
elements.sent-2411-217: Since the representation is crossing free, every intersection point must correspond to a vertex.
elements.sent-2411-218: Therefore, we construct the graph $G$ having as vertex set $E$ and distinct $e,e'\in E$ are adjacent if and only if they share a vertex.
elements.sent-2411-219: Clearly, $H$ admits a crossing-free line representation if and only if $G$ is complete $k$-partite for some $k\in\mathbb{N}$, which can be tested in polynomial time by checking whether each connected component in the complement graph of $G$ is a clique.
elements.sent-2411-220: For line segments, we first present hardness results and then cases that we solve, in particular this will include classes of hypergraphs that always have a crossing-free segment representation.
elements.sent-2411-221: The reduction in the proof of \cref{thm:erlinesgmentsrank3 constructs a hypergraph that admits a crossing-free segment representation if and only if the corresponding pseudoline arrangement is stretchable.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Thus, the same reduction gives the following result.
elements.sent-2411-223: It is $\exists\RR$-hard to decide whether a rank-$3$ max-degree-$6$ hypergraph has a crossing-free segment representation.
elements.sent-2411-224: The same holds for the reduction in the proof of \cref{thm:erlinesgmentsmdeg2 , which gives the following.}
elements.sent-2411-225: It is $\exists\RR$-hard to decide whether a rank-$5$ max-degree-$2$ hypergraph has a crossing-free segment representation.

(b) A strict crossing-free segment representation of the extended Pappus gadget.} \label{fig:ercrossingfreestrictlinesegments}

elements.sent-2411-251: A correspondence between strict crossing-free segment representations of degree-$2$ hypergraphs and segment intersection graphs gives the following result.
elements.sent-2411-252: We reduce from the problem segment intersection graph recognition, which is $\exists\RR$-hard \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/Matousek14 .}
elements.sent-2411-253: This problem is given a simple graph $G$ and asks whether there exists an arrangement $\mathcal{A}$ of segments in the plane such that $G$ is the intersection graph of $\mathcal{A}$, i.e., the vertices of $G$ correspond to segments of $\mathcal{A}$, and two vertices are connected by an edge if and only if the corresponding segments share at least one point.
elements.sent-2411-254: In that case, we say that $\mathcal{A}$ represents $G$.
elements.sent-2411-255: Clearly, we can assume that $G$ is connected and has at least two vertices.
elements.sent-2411-256: We construct a hypergraph $H$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-257: For each edge $e$ in $G$ we add a vertex $v_e$ to $H$.
elements.sent-2411-258: For each vertex $v$ in $G$ that is incident to edges $e_1,e_2,\dots,e_\ell$ we add the hyperedge $\{v_{e_1 ,v_{e_2 ,\dots,v_{e_\ell}\}$ to $H$.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: Notice that $H$ has degree two as each edge in $G$ is incident to two vertices.
elements.sent-2411-260: We show that $G$ is a segment intersection graph if and only if $H$ has a strict crossing-free segment representation.
elements.sent-2411-261: As $G$ is simple, we can assume that no two segments in $\mathcal{A}$ lie on the same line (see also \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/Matousek14 ).}
elements.sent-2411-262: Thus, if two line segments share a non-empty intersection, this intersection consists of a single point.
elements.sent-2411-263: Furthermore, with standard perturbation arguments we can assume that no three line segments in $\mathcal{A}$ intersect at a single point (see e.g.~\cite[Section 5]{eppstein2009testing ).}
elements.sent-2411-264: It is now easy to turn $\mathcal{A}$ into a strict crossing-free segment representation $(\alpha,\beta)$ .
elements.sent-2411-265: For vertices $v_e$ in $H$, $\alpha(v_e)$ is simply the unique intersection point of the two line segments corresponding to the two incident vertices of $e$.
elements.sent-2411-266: For the hyperedge $\hat{e =\{v_{e_1 ,v_{e_2 ,\dots,v_{e_\ell}\}$, $\beta(\hat{e )$ is the line segment in the line arrangement passing through $\alpha(v_{e_1 ),\alpha(v_{e_2 ),\dots,\alpha(v_{e_\ell )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-267: Notice that when interpreting the representation as line segment arrangement $\mathcal{A}$, then $\mathcal{A}$ represents $G$.
elements.sent-2411-268: We now present some cases where the existence of a crossing-free segment representation can be decided in polynomial time.
elements.sent-2411-269: The rank-2 case corresponds to deciding whether a graph is planar.
elements.sent-2411-270: Then $H$ is a simple graph, and every crossing-free segment representation can be made strict.
elements.sent-2411-271: Furthermore, $H$ has such a representation if and only if it is planar.
elements.sent-2411-272: We now present some hypergraph classes for which we can decide the existence of a strict crossing-free segment representation in polynomial time.
elements.sent-2411-273: The first class is based on \revision{permutation graphs which are graphs with vertex set $\{v_1,\dots,v_n\}$ and there exists a permutation $\pi$ such that $v_i,v_j$, $i<j$, are adjacent if and only if $i$ comes after $j$ in $\pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-274: If $G$ is a permutation graph (which is verifiable in linear time), then $H$ admits a crossing-free strict segment representation.. \end{restatable.}
elements.sent-2411-275: If $G$ is a permutation graph, then by definition $G$ admits a representation $\Gamma$ with segments such that two segments intersect if and only if the corresponding vertices are adjacent in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-276: Furthermore, the segments lie between a pair of parallel lines $\ell_u$ and $\ell_b$, where each segment has one endpoint on $\ell_u$ and the other endpoint on $\ell_b$.
elements.sent-2411-277: A permutation graph can be recognized, and its segment intersection representation can be computed in linear time~\cite{mcconnell1999modular .}
elements.sent-2411-278: Note that perturbation of the endpoints on $\ell_u$ does not change the intersection representation as long as the order of the endpoints on $\ell_u$ is preserved, but can ensure that no three segments meet at a single point.
elements.sent-2411-279: Consider now the segments of $\Gamma$ as the hyperedges of $H$.
elements.sent-2411-280: Every vertex $v$ with degree two in $H$ can be represented at the intersection point of the hyperedges in $\Gamma$ that contain $v$.
elements.sent-2411-281: Finally, the vertices that are unique to a hyperedge can be placed by choosing distinct points on the segment representing that hyperedge.
elements.sent-2411-282: The vertex-edge incidence graph of a hypergraph $H=(V,E)$ is a bipartite graph $G=(V \cup E, E')$, where an edge $(v,e)\in E'$, $v\in V$ and $e\in E$, exists, if and only if $e$ contains $v$.
elements.sent-2411-284: If $G$ has a planar embedding with vertex set $E$ on the outerface, then we can decide whether $H$ admits a crossing-free strict segment representation in polynomial time.. \end{restatable.}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{./graphics/osh.pdf} \caption{Illustration for \cref{lem:onesided}. Construction of a crossing-free strict segment representation in \cref{lem:onesided}} \label{fig:onesided}
elements.sent-2411-285: Since $G$ is a bipartite graph, the girth (i.e., the length of a shortest cycle) of $G$ is at least $4$.
elements.sent-2411-286: If $G$ is of girth 4 then there are two hyperedges $e,e'$ in $H$ that contain at least two vertices in common.
elements.sent-2411-287: Therefore, $H$ cannot have a strict line segment representation.
elements.sent-2411-288: We can check whether the girth of $G$ 4 in linear time~\cite{chang2013computing .}
elements.sent-2411-289: If the girth of $G$ is at least 6, then we can construct a crossing-free strict segment representation as described below.
elements.sent-2411-290: Let $\Gamma$ be an embedding of $G$ where all the vertices of $E$ are on the outerface.
elements.sent-2411-291: Such an embedding is straightforward to construct using a planar graph embedding algorithm, e.g., if we add a vertex $w$ to $G$ and make it adjacent to all the vertices of $E$, then a planar graph embedding brings all the vertices of $E$ in the same face which can then be chosen as the outerface to obtain $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-292: The embedding $\Gamma$ can be used to construct a straight-line planar drawing $\Gamma'$ where each vertex in $E$ lies on a horizontal line $L$, each vertex in $V$ appears strictly above $L$ with the $x$-coordinate lying between its leftmost and rightmost neighbours, and each edge in $E'$ drawn with a straight line segment between the corresponding endpoints (\cref{fig:onesided (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-293: Such a drawing can be computed in polynomial time~\cite{kaufmann2002embedding .}
elements.sent-2411-294: We repeatedly find the set of vertices $S$ in $\Gamma'$ (i.e., vertices and hyperedges of $H$) that lie on the unbounded region above $L$ and create points and segments to represent them.
elements.sent-2411-295: For example, in \cref{fig:onesided (a), we the selected set is $S=\{e_1,a,e_7,b,c,d,e_{13 ,e_{16}\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-296: Let $S_i$ be the set selected at that $i$th iteration.
elements.sent-2411-297: Let $\ell_s$ be the line determined by a line segment $s$.
elements.sent-2411-298: We will use a drawing invariant that the leftmost and rightmost hyperedges $e,e'$ of $S_i$ are already drawn with distinct line segments $s,s'$ and the rest of the hyperedges of $S_i$ would be drawn using a convex chain in the region bounded by the lines $\ell_s, \ell_{s'}$.
elements.sent-2411-299: For $S_1$, we draw the leftmost and rightmost hyperedges $e,e'$ using segments $s,s'$ of positive and negative slopes, respectively, so that their corresponding lines form a convex chain (\cref{fig:onesided (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-300: If there is a vertex in $S$ that is incident to both $e,e'$, then we ensure that $s$ and $s'$ intersect, otherwise, we keep them disjoint.
elements.sent-2411-301: Let $e_1(=e),\ldots,e_t(=e')$ be the hyperedges of $H$ in $S$ which are in this left to right order on $L$.
elements.sent-2411-302: We draw $e_2,\ldots,e_{t-1}$ using a convex polygonal chain between $e,e'$ and place the vertices of $S$ on the chain to realize the necessary adjacencies.
elements.sent-2411-303: For each vertex $v$ on $S$, we now draw the hyperedges adjacent to it by splitting the angle created by leftmost and rightmost hyperedges, e.g., see vertex $a$ in \cref{fig:onesided (e)--(f).}
elements.sent-2411-305: Note that some hyperedges of $S_i$ are already drawn and we can use them to find maximal sequences in $S_i$ where the leftmost and rightmost hyperedges are already drawn.
elements.sent-2411-306: For example, \cref{fig:onesided (g) shows the maximal sequences for $S_2$ in red, green, orange and purple.}
elements.sent-2411-307: For each maximal sequence $e_p,\ldots,e_q$, where $1\le p,q \le |E|$, we draw the sequence with a convex chain between the segments representing $e_p$ and $e_q$.
elements.sent-2411-308: However, we also need to ensure the drawing remains crossing-free.
elements.sent-2411-309: If $S_{i-1}$ contains a vertex that is adjacent to both $e_p$ and $S_q$, then $S_i$ cannot contain a vertex adjacent to both $e_p$ and $e_q$ (otherwise, the girth of $G$ would be 4).
elements.sent-2411-310: Therefore, we can draw $e_p,\ldots,e_q$ such that the drawing remains crossing-free.
elements.sent-2411-311: The representation that we construct in Lemma~\ref{lem:onesided can be seen as a contact system of segments, and hence this identifies a class of graphs for which the existence of segment contact representation can be tested in polynomial time without using the conditions of~\cite{de2007representation,DBLP:journals/algorithmica/FraysseixM07 that check some properties over all subsystems of at least two paths.}}
elements.sent-2411-312: In this section, we discuss rank-$3$ max-degree-$3$ hypergraphs and whether they have line representations with few bends.
elements.sent-2411-313: Indeed, the PhD thesis of Ernst Steinitz \cite{steinitz1894konstruction claims that every 3-uniform 3-regular hypergraph can be represented with one line per hyperedge, except maybe one hyperedge (which could be represented with one bend).}
elements.sent-2411-314: However, more careful consideration shows that this is indeed not true, as already pointed out by Grünbaum~\cite{DBLP:books/ph/Gruenbaum09 .}
elements.sent-2411-315: Similar results exist, but none show the claim of Steinitz \cite{glynnRepresentationConfigurationsProjective2000,kocay1999application .}
elements.sent-2411-316: We show a construction that has at least two hyperedges that must have a bend, and generalize this construction.
elements.sent-2411-317: For this, we define, for $t\in \mathbb{N _0$, a strict $t$-bend line representation for a hypergraph as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-318: In the original definition of line representations, we replace lines by what we call infinite polygonal chains.
elements.sent-2411-319: An infinite polygonal chain consists of a (possibly empty) polygonal chain and two rays, one ending at the first point of the chain, one ending at the last point (see \cref{fig:polychain ).}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=.9\textwidth]{./graphics/infpolychain.pdf} \caption{An infinite polygonal chain with $3$ bends extending infinitely to the left and right.} \label{fig:polychain}
elements.sent-2411-320: Essentially, an infinite polygonal chain is a polyline whose first and last segment is extended infinitely.
elements.sent-2411-321: Further, two distinct infinite polygonal chains $\beta(e),\beta(e')$ for $e,e'\in E$ must not share a line segment nor a bend point.
elements.sent-2411-322: Lastly, we require that the total number of all bends in $\beta(E)$ is exactly $t$, where $\beta(E)=\{\beta(e)\mid e\in E\}$.
elements.sent-2411-323: Consider the connected 3-uniform 3-regular hypergraph $H$ defined as follows.
elements.sent-2411-324: Let $H_P^1$ and $H_P^2$ be two Pappus gadgets with vertices $p_1,\dots,p_9$ and $p_1',\dots,p_9'$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-325: The hypergraph $H$ is the union of $H_P^1,H_P^2$, and the hyperedges $\{p_7',p_8,p_9\}$ and $\{p_7,p_8',p_9'\}$.
elements.sent-2411-327: There is no $t$-bend representation for $H$ with $t<2$.
elements.sent-2411-328: If every hyperedge in the subhypergraph $H_P^2$ is represented without a bend, then $\beta(\{p_7',p_8,p_9\})$ must pass through $p_7$ due to \cref{thm:pappusthm .}
elements.sent-2411-329: Thus, at least one hyperedge of $H_P^1$ must be represented with at least one bend.
elements.sent-2411-330: Applying the same argument to $H_P^2$, we see that we require at least two bends.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{./graphics/pappusgadgetproofbend.pdf} \caption{A 3-uniform 3-regular hypergraph requiring at least 4 bends in a strict line representation.} \label{fig:manybends}
elements.sent-2411-331: The above construction can be generalized so that there is no strict $t$-bend line representation for any $t<x$ for an arbitrary $x\in\mathbb{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-332: Instead of one copy of $H_P^1$, we add $x-1$ copies $H_P^2,H_P^3,\dots,H_P^x$.
elements.sent-2411-334: Further, we add $x$ hyperedges $e_1,e_2,\dots,e_x$ such that $e_i$ contains $p_8,p_9$ of $H_P^i$ and $p_7$ of $H_P^{(i \mod x) + 1}$.
elements.sent-2411-335: For any $x\in \mathbb{N}$ there exists a connected rank-3 hypergraph $H$ such that there exists no strict $t$-bend representation for $H$ for any $t<x$.
elements.sent-2411-336: Similar constructions exist (at least for $x=2$) \cite{DBLP:books/ph/Gruenbaum09 , the generalization has not been stated explicitly.}
elements.sent-2411-337: We studied representations of hypergraphs by arrangements of lines/segments.
elements.sent-2411-338: While we answered several complexity questions, many open problems remain:.
elements.sent-2411-339: What is the time complexity of deciding whether a hypergraph has a crossing-free line representation?.
elements.sent-2411-340: We have almost characterized the complexity of deciding whether a max-degree-2 hypergraph has a segment representation (hardness for rank at least five and a polynomial algorithm for rank at most three).
elements.sent-2411-342: As most straight-line variants are hard, it is a natural question to explore the space where curves can have bends.

elements.sent-2411-343: Firman and Spoerhase~\cite{DBLP:journals/dmtcs/FirmanS25 consider special representations of $k$-uniform hypergraphs, where each vertex is an axis-aligned affine $\ell$-dimensional plane in $\RR^d$ ($d>\ell$ and $k=\binom{d {\ell}$) and each hyperedge is a point in $\RR^d$ that is part of the planes defined by its incident vertices.}}
elements.sent-2411-344: They give a characterization by vertex cuts and give a polynomial-time algorithm to test whether a hypergraph has such a representation.
elements.sent-2411-345: The authors of \cite{DBLP:conf/gd/BertschingerMKMW23 consider the problem of representing hypergraphs such that vertices are points in $\RR^d$ and hyperedges are bi-curved, difference-separable, convex sets cointaining their incident points.}
elements.sent-2411-346: They show $\exists\RR$-hardness of the associated recognition problems.
elements.sent-2411-347: It is $\exists\RR$-hard to decide whether a rank-$3$ max-degree-$16$ hypergraph has a strict segment representation.
elements.sent-2411-348: Indeed, the PhD thesis of Ernst Steinitz \cite{steinitz1894konstruction claims that every $3$-uniform $3$-regular hypergraph can be represented with one line per hyperedge, except maybe one hyperedge (which could be represented with one bend).}
